channel.title: Whamcloud Community JIRA
channel.description: This file is an XML representation of an issue

item.title: [LU-20034] osc: unconditional wake_up on osc_lru_waitq causes severe spinlock contention in fadvise path
item.description:
wake_up(&osc_lru_waitq) is called unconditionally from
osc_lru_unreserve() on every page release. When the VFS
fadvise(POSIX_FADV_DONTNEED) path triggers page invalidation
via ll_releasepage -> osc_page_delete, this results in one
wake_up call per page, each taking the waitqueue spinlock.

With many cores calling fadvise in parallel from many
threads, as rclone does, this causes catastrophic spinlock
contention. Observed on a 256-core customer system running
rclone with 64 parallel transfers copying 2TB of 10GB
files over Lustre:

  • 69% of all CPU cycles spent in
    native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath, almost entirely from
    __wake_up_common_lock in the fadvise -> osc_page_delete
    path
  • rclone consuming 6500% CPU (65 cores), nearly all in
    kernel spinlock contention
  • 29% system CPU across 256 cores (~74 cores in kernel)
  • Effective throughput ~4.8 GB/s read + 4.8 GB/s write,
    well below what the hardware (40+ SSD OSTs over IB)
    can deliver

The I/O pattern itself is reasonable: 100% full-size 1MB
RPCs (256 pages), sequential access, healthy RPC latencies
(1-20ms). The bottleneck is entirely CPU-side.

The rclone copy pattern is: read 1MB -> write 1MB ->
fadvise(DONTNEED) on source. The fadvise invalidates
pages one at a time, each calling wake_up on the global
osc_lru_waitq. With ~89 threads hitting this path
concurrently on 256 cores, the single waitqueue spinlock
becomes a serialization point.

Calling fadvise on every block is wasteful - it would be
much better to let the file system handle cache eviction -
and unfortunately the kernel fadvise machinery is
single-page, which is very inefficient for Lustre's
per-page teardown path.

Fix: guard wake_up with waitqueue_active() so the
spinlock is only taken when there are actual waiters,
and consolidate the wake_up into osc_lru_unreserve()
so there is a single call site.
item.summary: osc: unconditional wake_up on osc_lru_waitq causes severe spinlock contention in fadvise path

status.description: A resolution has been taken, and it is awaiting verification by reporter. From here issues are either reopened, or are closed.

comments.comment:
"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/64790/
Subject: LU-20034 osc: severe spinlock contention in fadvise path
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: 1223b674ef0a66bb82c3b928ada02b811ee10d11
Nice. Always best to fix things at the source, when possible, rather than trying to optimize poorly-behaving callers. .
(That's me, but there was an existing discussion of doing so)

Only by replacing fadvise and providing our own implementation - we use the generic_fadvise today, and it runs through some fairly basic kernel machinery for page invalidation.  I'd rather not change that - that machinery is what works on a single page at a time.

I realized after writing this the issue is rclone is calling fdavise over and over from 64 threads, rather than letting kswapd handle page freeing.  So the lock is thrashing mightily.

There's an rclone PR to remove fadvise entirely, since it seems to have been put in without reason.  Will link shortly.

stancheff, paf0186 Is there some way to improve how fadvise() is invalidating pages? Is that fixed in the folio world to invalidate whole folios? Reducing the per-page invalidation overhead is good, but even better to process many pages at once...
